title: Strong/Weak Mastery and Salt Hydrolysis
subtitle: Consolidation: fix the errors, predict the salt
subject: Chemistry
syllabusDotPoints: construct models and/or animations to communicate the differences between strong, weak, concentrated and dilute acids and bases (ACSCH099)
inquiryQuestion: What is the role of water in solutions of acids and bases?
lessonIntent: Students can diagnose the four common strong/weak errors and predict whether any salt solution is acidic, basic or neutral.
examSkill: Apply the salt hydrolysis rule: identify the parent acid and base, classify each as strong or weak, and predict the salt solution's pH with the correct ionic equation.

introVoiceover.text: Welcome to HSC Science. This is HSC Chemistry. Today, strong and weak mastery, and predicting salt solutions. Let's get into it.

scenes.caption: Strong/Weak Mastery and Salt Hydrolysis (Year 12 Chemistry, Module 6, Lesson 6)

scenes.heading: Only one student is right
scenes.body: In 2022, NESA flagged that most students confused acid strength with concentration, or wrote the wrong arrow.
scenes.callout: Four answers to one question. Can you diagnose the three wrong ones?
scenes.caption: A real HSC marking note: strength-vs-concentration confusion is the top error.

voiceover.text: OK, this lesson is a consolidation, so no brand-new dot points, just sharpening what you already know until it's exam-proof. Here's the motivation. In the twenty twenty-two HSC chemistry exam, there was a question asking students to explain the difference between strong and weak acids. And NESA's own marking notes said most students either confused strength with concentration, or wrote the wrong arrow for a strong acid. Imagine four students answering the same question, and only one of them is fully correct. Today you'll learn to diagnose every wrong answer precisely, and then we'll add a powerful new skill, predicting whether any salt dissolves to give an acidic, basic, or neutral solution.

scenes.heading: The four errors, fixed
scenes.body: Almost every lost mark in this topic is one of four predictable mistakes.

bullets.text: Wrong arrow: writing ⇌ for a strong acid. Strong acids take →.
Dilute means weak: no. HCl is strong at any concentration.
Using [H⁺] = c for a weak acid: it's only partly ionised, so [H⁺] is far lower.

scenes.secondary: The fourth error is naming only NaOH as a strong base. KOH, Ca(OH)₂ and Ba(OH)₂ are all strong too.
scenes.callout: Run a quick check: right arrow, separate descriptors, right method, full list.
scenes.caption: Four errors: wrong arrow, dilute≠weak, [H⁺]=c for weak, incomplete strong-base list.

voiceover.text: Let's nail the four errors. Error one, writing the equilibrium arrow for a strong acid, like hydrochloric acid in balance with its ions. Strong acids ionise completely, so they take a single forward arrow, and the wrong arrow loses the mark every time. Error two, treating dilute as a synonym for weak. Hydrochloric acid is strong at any concentration, so a dilute sample is a dilute strong acid, never a weak one. Error three, calculating a weak acid's pH as if hydrogen ion concentration equals the acid concentration. A weak acid is only partly ionised, so its hydrogen ion concentration is far lower, and you need a Ka method, which is the next lessons. Error four, naming only sodium hydroxide as a strong base. Potassium, calcium, and barium hydroxide are all strong too. Run those four as a checklist before you submit anything.

scenes.heading: Salt hydrolysis: predicting the pH
scenes.body: A salt's solution pH depends on whether its ions came from a strong or weak parent.

bullets.text: Conjugate of a strong acid or base, like Cl⁻ or Na⁺, is a neutral spectator.
Conjugate of a weak acid, like CH₃COO⁻, is basic: it grabs H⁺ from water.
Conjugate of a weak base, like NH₄⁺, is acidic: it donates H⁺ to water.

scenes.secondary: So neutralisation only gives a neutral salt when BOTH parents are strong. NaCl is neutral, CH₃COONa is basic, NH₄Cl is acidic.
scenes.callout: Trace each ion back to its parent acid or base to predict the pH.
scenes.caption: Conjugate of strong = neutral; of weak acid = basic; of weak base = acidic.

voiceover.text: Now the new skill, and it's genuinely useful, salt hydrolysis. Every salt dissolves into ions, and whether those ions disturb the pH depends on where they came from. Here's the rule in three lines. An ion that's the conjugate of a strong acid or a strong base, like chloride or sodium, is just a neutral spectator, it does nothing. An ion that's the conjugate of a weak acid, like acetate, is basic, because it pulls a proton off water and leaves hydroxide behind. And an ion that's the conjugate of a weak base, like ammonium, is acidic, because it donates a proton to water. The big consequence, neutralisation only gives a neutral salt when both the parent acid and base are strong. So table salt is neutral, sodium acetate is basic, and ammonium chloride is acidic.

scenes.heading: The words you need

bullets.text: Degree of ionisation: the fraction of molecules that have ionised.
Salt hydrolysis: a salt's ions reacting with water to shift the pH.
Spectator ion: an ion that doesn't react, leaving pH unchanged.
Basic salt: dissolves above pH 7; acidic salt: dissolves below pH 7.

scenes.callout: The word in 'neutralisation' is about the process, not always a neutral pH.
scenes.caption: Degree of ionisation, salt hydrolysis, spectator ion, basic vs acidic salt.

voiceover.text: Quick vocabulary. Degree of ionisation is the fraction of molecules that have actually ionised, your precise measure of strength. Salt hydrolysis is when a salt's ions react with water to push the pH up or down. A spectator ion is one that doesn't react at all, so it leaves the pH untouched, like chloride or sodium. A basic salt dissolves to give a solution above pH seven, and an acidic salt gives one below seven. And here's a subtle point that trips people up, the word neutralisation refers to the process of combining an acid and a base. It does not guarantee a neutral product. Only when both parents are strong does neutralisation actually give you a pH-seven solution.

scenes.heading: Predict the salt pH
scenes.question: Predict acidic, basic or neutral for: (a) NaNO₃, (b) NH₄Cl, (c) CH₃COONa.
scenes.coachNote: Trace each ion to its parent acid and base, then apply the rule.
steps: (a) NaNO₃: Na⁺ from NaOH (strong) and NO₃⁻ from HNO₃ (strong) → both spectators → neutral
(b) NH₄Cl: Cl⁻ from HCl (strong) is a spectator; NH₄⁺ from NH₃ (weak base) is acidic
NH₄⁺ + H₂O ⇌ NH₃ + H₃O⁺ donates H⁺ → solution is acidic (pH < 7)
(c) CH₃COONa: Na⁺ is a spectator; CH₃COO⁻ from CH₃COOH (weak acid) is basic
Answer: NaNO₃ neutral; NH₄Cl acidic; CH₃COONa basic ✓
scenes.caption: NaNO₃ neutral; NH₄Cl acidic; CH₃COONa basic.

voiceover.text: Let's predict three salt solutions. Part a, sodium nitrate. Sodium comes from sodium hydroxide, a strong base, and nitrate comes from nitric acid, a strong acid, so both are spectators, and the solution is neutral. Part b, ammonium chloride. Chloride comes from hydrochloric acid, a strong acid, so it's a spectator. But ammonium comes from ammonia, a weak base, so it's acidic, donating a proton to water as ammonium plus water in balance with ammonia plus hydronium, giving a pH below seven. Part c, sodium acetate. Sodium is again a spectator, but acetate comes from acetic acid, a weak acid, so it's basic, pulling a proton off water. So sodium nitrate is neutral, ammonium chloride is acidic, and sodium acetate is basic. Every time, just trace each ion back to its parent.

scenes.heading: Same concentration, different pH
scenes.question: 0.10 mol L⁻¹ HCl reads pH 1.0; 0.10 mol L⁻¹ HNO₂ reads pH 2.1. Explain, and why does HCl conduct more?
scenes.coachNote: Same concentration, so any difference is degree of ionisation.
steps: HCl is strong → 100% ionised → [H⁺] = 0.10 mol L⁻¹ → pH 1.0
HNO₂ is weak → only partly ionised → [H⁺] ≈ 0.0079 mol L⁻¹ → pH 2.1
Conductivity tracks total ion count; HCl makes ~0.20 mol L⁻¹ ions vs HNO₂'s ~0.016
Answer: same concentration, different pH and conductivity, from different ionisation ✓
scenes.caption: HCl 100% ionised (pH 1.0); HNO₂ ~7.9% ionised (pH 2.1); HCl conducts far more.

voiceover.text: Now a compare-and-explain. Nought-point-one molar hydrochloric acid reads pH one, and nought-point-one molar nitrous acid reads pH two-point-one. Both at the same concentration, so the difference is purely strength. Step one, hydrochloric acid is strong, so it's a hundred percent ionised, giving the full nought-point-one molar hydrogen ions and pH one. Step two, nitrous acid is weak, so it's only partly ionised, giving about nought-point-zero-zero-seven-nine molar hydrogen ions, hence pH two-point-one. Step three, that's a degree of ionisation of about seven-point-nine percent. Step four, conductivity follows the total number of ions, and hydrochloric acid makes about nought-point-two molar of ions while nitrous makes only about nought-point-zero-one-six, so hydrochloric conducts far more. Same concentration, different pH and different conductivity, both flowing from the same root cause, complete versus partial ionisation.

scenes.heading: Three traps
scenes.body: Trap 1: assuming every salt is neutral because it came from neutralisation. Only strong-plus-strong gives neutral.
scenes.secondary: Trap 2: calling Ca(OH)₂ weak because limewater's pH is modest. It's a sparingly soluble strong base; low solubility, not weakness. Trap 3: thinking a concentrated weak acid can't out-acidify a dilute strong one. It can, because [H⁺] depends on both strength and concentration.
scenes.callout: Not all salts are neutral; solubility isn't strength; concentration matters too.
scenes.caption: Salts aren't all neutral; Ca(OH)₂ is strong but sparingly soluble; concentrated weak can beat dilute strong.

voiceover.text: Three traps. Trap one, assuming every salt gives a neutral solution because it was made by neutralisation. Only when both the acid and the base are strong is the salt neutral. Ammonium chloride is acidic, sodium acetate is basic. The word neutralisation describes the process, not the product's pH. Trap two, calling calcium hydroxide weak because limewater's pH isn't sky-high. Calcium hydroxide is a strong base, the dissolved part dissociates completely. Its modest hydroxide level comes from low solubility, only about nought-point-zero-two molar dissolves. Solubility and strength are different axes. Trap three, believing a concentrated weak acid can't beat a dilute strong one on pH. It absolutely can, because hydrogen ion concentration depends on both strength and concentration, and enough molecules of a weak acid can overwhelm a tiny amount of a strong one.

scenes.heading: Quick check
scenes.question: Which of NaCl, NH₄NO₃, and Na₂CO₃ gives a basic solution, and why?
answerSteps: NaCl: Na⁺ (strong base) and Cl⁻ (strong acid) → both spectators → neutral
NH₄NO₃: NH₄⁺ (weak base NH₃) acidic; NO₃⁻ (strong acid) spectator → acidic
Na₂CO₃: Na⁺ spectator; CO₃²⁻ from H₂CO₃ (weak acid) is basic
CO₃²⁻ + H₂O ⇌ HCO₃⁻ + OH⁻ releases OH⁻ → pH > 7
scenes.caption: Na₂CO₃ is basic: CO₃²⁻ is the conjugate of weak H₂CO₃ and releases OH⁻.

voiceover.text: Quick check. Of sodium chloride, ammonium nitrate, and sodium carbonate, which gives a basic solution, and why? Pause and work it through. Right, let's check it. Sodium chloride, sodium from a strong base and chloride from a strong acid, both spectators, so it's neutral. Ammonium nitrate, ammonium from the weak base ammonia is acidic, and nitrate from strong nitric acid is a spectator, so the solution is acidic. Sodium carbonate, sodium is a spectator, but carbonate comes from carbonic acid, a weak acid, so it's basic, reacting as carbonate plus water in balance with hydrogen carbonate plus hydroxide, releasing hydroxide and pushing the pH above seven. So the basic one is sodium carbonate. That's exactly why washing soda is used as a base around the house.

scenes.heading: Lock it in
points: Four classic errors: wrong arrow, dilute≠weak, [H⁺]=c for weak, short base list.
Conjugate of a strong parent is a neutral spectator.
Conjugate of a weak acid is basic; conjugate of a weak base is acidic.
Neutralisation gives a neutral salt only when both parents are strong.
Trace each ion to its parent acid or base to predict the salt's pH.
scenes.finalPrompt: Given a salt? Identify both parents, classify each, apply the rule.
scenes.caption: Five takeaways: the four errors, the hydrolysis rule, strong+strong=neutral, trace the ions.

voiceover.text: Let's lock it in. Five things to take away. First, the four classic errors, the wrong arrow, treating dilute as weak, using hydrogen ion concentration equals concentration for a weak acid, and an incomplete strong-base list. Second, the conjugate of a strong acid or base is a neutral spectator. Third, the conjugate of a weak acid is basic, and the conjugate of a weak base is acidic. Fourth, neutralisation only produces a neutral salt when both parents are strong. And fifth, to predict any salt's pH, just trace each ion back to its parent acid or base and apply the rule. Next lesson, we go deeper on conjugate pairs, amphiprotic salts, and water's special role. See you there.
